perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The Agency may terminate this agreement at any time by notice in writing if; a) the Contractor becomes insolvent, ceases to carry out its business, has a receiver, liquidator, administrative r eceiver, administrator, trustee or other similar officer appointed over the whole or part of its assets or an order is made or a resolution passed for the winding up of the Contractor (save for a solvent winding up as part of a bona fide reconstruction or amalgamation); or b) the Contractor commits a breach of this agreement or of the Contract which is material and not capable of remedy or which is capable of remedy but which is not remedied within 60 days of notice to the Agency.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The Agency may terminate this agreement at any time by notice in writing if; a) the Contractor becomes insolvent, ceases to carry out its business, has a receiver, liquidator, administrative r eceiver, administrator, trustee or other similar officer appointed over the whole or part of its assets or an order is made or a resolution passed for the winding up of the Contractor (save for a solvent winding up as part of a bona fide reconstruction or amalgamation); or b) the Contractor commits a breach of this agreement or of the Contract which is material and not capable of remedy or which is capable of remedy but which is not remedied within 10 days of notice to the Agency.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the remedy period from 60 days to 10 days for a material breach may conflict with French law, which often requires a reasonable period for remedy based on the nature of the breach.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1225 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1225 of the French Civil Code implies that a reasonable period must be granted to remedy a breach before termination, especially when the breach is remediable. Reducing the cure period to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the nature and complexity of the breach, making the termination clause potentially unenforceable under French law.

perturbation.justification: The change from 60 days to 10 days for remedying a material breach creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that Article 1225 of the French Civil Code implies that a reasonable period must be granted to remedy a breach before termination, especially when the breach is remediable. The Perturbation Explanation elaborates, stating that reducing the remedy period from 60 days to 10 days may conflict with French law, which often requires a reasonable period for remedy based on the nature of the breach. The scraped snippets do not provide specific laws. However, based on the other information given, the altered text creates a contradiction by potentially violating the 'reasonable period' requirement under French law.

perturbation.original_text: 5.2 Notification sent under this agreement shall be deemed valid and effective when received by mail, fax or e -mail providi ng there is written confirmation of receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 Notification sent under this agreement shall be deemed valid and effective when sent by mail, fax or e -mail, regardless of whether there is written confirmation of receipt.
perturbation.explanation: Under French law, especially in contractual matters, proof of receipt of notification is often required for it to be considered valid, particularly for important notices like those related to termination or breach. Removing the requirement for written confirmation of receipt may conflict with standard legal practice in France.
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1367 of the French Civil Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1367 of the French Civil Code emphasizes the importance of proof in legal matters. By eliminating the requirement for written confirmation of receipt, the modified clause could be seen as conflicting with the need to establish that the notification was actually received by the intended recipient, potentially rendering the notification ineffective under French law.
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for written confirmation of receipt for notifications, making them effective upon sending. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this removal conflicts with French legal practice and Article 1367 of the French Civil Code, which emphasizes the importance of proof in legal matters. By eliminating proof of receipt, the modified clause weakens the ability to establish that the notification was received, creating a legal contradiction. While the scraped snippets are missing, the LLM-provided explanations strongly indicate a conflict with French legal standards regarding proof of notification.

perturbation.original_text: 8.2 All disputes arising out of or in connection with this agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one or more arbitrators appoi nted in accordance with the said Rules.
perturbation.changed_text: 8.2 All disputes arising out of or in connection with this agreement shall be finally settled under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce by one arbitrator appointed solely by the Agency.
perturbation.explanation: Specifying that only the Agency appoints the arbitrator could violate principles of fairness and impartiality required by French arbitration law, potentially rendering the arbitration clause unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: French Code of Civil Procedure, Article 1456
perturbation.law_citation: Article 1456 of the French Code of Civil Procedure
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1456 of the French Code of Civil Procedure emphasizes the independence and impartiality of arbitrators. Granting the Agency sole power to appoint the arbitrator undermines these principles, as it creates a potential bias and could be seen as violating the right to a fair hearing, making the arbitration agreement vulnerable to challenge under French law.
perturbation.justification: The modification in the contract text, granting the Agency sole power to appoint the arbitrator, directly contradicts the principles of fairness and impartiality emphasized for arbitration proceedings under French law. The Law Explanation explicitly states that Article 1456 of the French Code of Civil Procedure prioritizes arbitrator independence and impartiality. By allowing the Agency to unilaterally appoint the arbitrator, the changed text introduces a potential bias, undermining the right to a fair hearing, thus creating a significant legal contradiction. While Scraped Law Snippets are unavailable, the Law Explanation provides sufficient grounds to consider this a legal contradiction, as it describes how the change violates specific principles of French arbitration law.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1 At the request of the supplier of the Proprietary Information the receiving party shall immediately return all Proprietary Information which the receiving party (or an Authorised Person/Agency Authorised Person) has in its possession, custody or control including all materials (in hardcopy or electronic form) that incorporate any part of the Proprietary Information and, if required, destroy any copies of such Proprietary Information and materials and certify in writing that such destruction has been completed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1 At the request of the supplier of the Proprietary Information, the receiving party shall return all Proprietary Information within 90 days, and destruction of copies is optional.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of proprietary information to 90 days and making destruction of copies optional weakens the protection of proprietary information and may not align with the reasonable expectations of confidentiality under French commercial law. French law tends to emphasize prompt return and complete destruction to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_citation: Article L151-1 of the French Commercial Code
perturbation.law_explanation: Article L151-1 of the French Commercial Code deals with the protection of trade secrets. Delaying the return of proprietary information and making the destruction of copies optional can be seen as a failure to take reasonable steps to protect the confidentiality of trade secrets, potentially violating French law. The immediate return and complete destruction are generally expected to maintain confidentiality effectively.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction. The original text mandates immediate return of proprietary information and potential destruction of copies. The changed text weakens this by allowing a 90-day delay for return and making destruction optional. This directly contradicts the general expectation under French law that trade secrets are protected by prompt action, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, by failing to take reasonable steps to protect confidentiality of trade secrets.
